skill.name: infsh-cli
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover and run cloud AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D generation, and automation.
skill.summary: Run inference.sh AI apps from an AI assistant using the belt CLI.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis raised many command, network, credential, filesystem, and pipe-to-shell alerts. Most command and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown command examples and SHA-256 checksum documentation, but the skill intentionally enables authenticated belt CLI execution against external cloud services. Publish with clear warnings for network use, local file upload, API key handling, and social posting actions.

medium_findings.title: Authorized External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for belt commands and provides many examples that run remote inference.sh apps. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can trigger paid cloud jobs, send prompts to external providers, and act through the authenticated user account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated belt app run examples directly confirm external command execution. The behavior matches the stated purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local File Uploads to Remote Apps
medium_findings.description: The documentation says the CLI automatically uploads local files when a path is supplied. This can expose private images, audio, videos, or documents if the assistant runs a command with unintended file paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file upload behavior is described explicitly and supported with local path examples. It is a valid feature, but it creates a clear data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Account Context Use
medium_findings.description: The skill explains login, local credential storage, and the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable. Commands run with the user account context, so generated tasks and app actions may consume account resources or access configured secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly document authentication state and API key usage. No secret exfiltration was found, but account-scoped execution is confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Social Media Automation Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for posting to Twitter or X and installing a Twitter automation skill. These actions can publish content externally through the user account if run without careful approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly reference posting and media creation on X or Twitter. The risk depends on user authorization and configured credentials, so it is medium severity.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Recommendation
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends installing the CLI with a curl pipe to sh pattern. The file also gives a manual checksum path, and the allowed tool scope does not authorize curl, but users could still follow a risky installation pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is present in multiple installation sections. It is documentation for a vendor installer, not hidden execution, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer classified many Markdown inline code and fenced shell examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples, not executable Ruby code inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown command tables or fenced examples. No Ruby source file or dynamic command construction was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The SHA-256 reference describes checksum verification for a downloaded CLI binary. Static weak-crypto alerts on command tables and headings do not show use of MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only confirmed cryptographic reference is SHA-256 checksum verification, which is appropriate for integrity checking. Other cited locations are ordinary Markdown text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, installer, distribution, and example output locations. No evidence found of covert callbacks or unrelated exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or expected vendor service URLs. They still indicate network dependence, but not malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh appears as an installation option. Users should prefer the manual download and checksum verification path when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present. The risk is mitigated because it is installation documentation and the skill tool scope only allows belt commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Network CLI Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to run belt commands that call remote inference.sh apps. This can transmit prompts, files, and task metadata to external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Remote app execution and local file upload behavior are directly documented. The pattern is expected for the skill but requires user awareness.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps with inference.sh
content.value_statement: AI teams need a fast way to call many model providers without wiring each API by hand. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use the inference.sh belt CLI for discovery, execution, and task tracking.
actual_capabilities: Installs or references the belt CLI workflow for inference.sh cloud apps.
Discovers public and account apps by search, category, featured status, and details.
Runs image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and utility apps with inline or file inputs.
Generates sample input files and checks long-running task status.
Supports local file paths for uploads to apps that accept media URLs.
Documents authentication with login and the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an inference.sh account before most commands work.
Remote apps may require credits, provider availability, or configured account secrets.
Local files can be uploaded when file paths are used in inputs.
The skill documents many app names, but availability may change over time.

use_cases.title: Prototype multimodal AI workflows
use_cases.description: Run image, video, speech, search, and LLM apps from one CLI while testing workflow ideas.

use_cases.title: Create media assets from prompts
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, avatars, and audio outputs without managing separate provider SDKs.

use_cases.title: Operate inference tasks from CI
use_cases.description: Use API-key authentication, saved input files, task polling, and JSON output for automated jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inference.sh CLI guidance to find apps for image generation. Show me safe commands to inspect options before running anything.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a sample input
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me generate a sample input file for google/veo-3-1-fast, explain the fields, and wait before running the app.

prompt_templates.title: Run and track a task
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the selected inference.sh app with my approved input file, then track the task until it returns a result.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multimodal workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a workflow that searches the web, writes a prompt, generates an image, and creates a short video. Ask before each external command.

output_examples.input: Find image generation apps for a product mockup.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant app names, categories, and inspection commands. It asks for approval before submitting a paid or networked run.

output_examples.input: Run a video generation app and check the result later.
output_examples.output: The assistant starts an approved task, records the task identifier, checks status, and summarizes the final media result location.

output_examples.input: Use a local image for upscaling.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the exact local file path, explains that it will be uploaded, then runs the app only after approval.

best_practices: Inspect app details and sample inputs before running a remote task.
Confirm every local file path before uploading media to inference.sh apps.
Use API keys in environment variables only for trusted CI or controlled shells.
anti_patterns: Do not run app commands that post to social media without explicit approval.
Do not pass broad directories or sensitive local files as app inputs.
Do not pipe installer scripts to a shell when manual verification is practical.

faq.question: Does this skill run code on my machine?
faq.answer: It can run belt CLI commands through the assistant tool permission. The commands call external inference.sh services.

faq.question: Does it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes, if an app input uses a local file path. Confirm paths before running commands.

faq.question: Can it use Claude and OpenRouter models?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes OpenRouter and Claude model examples where the corresponding apps are available.

faq.question: Is an inference.sh account required?
faq.answer: Yes. Most runs require belt login or the INFSH_API_KEY environment variable.

faq.question: Can it post to Twitter or X?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples for X posting apps. Treat those commands as high-impact account actions.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it combines external commands, network services, credentials, and optional file uploads.
